figures.caption: Figure 4: The winning rate curves evaluated on the 6 academy Gfootball tasks among our ATD+MAPPO and other baselines.
Figure 3: The winning rate curves evaluated on the 6 SMAC tasks with two major difficulties among our ATD+QMIX and other baselines.
Table 1: Performance in Gfootball tasks within 50M time steps

Figure 1: (a): Two agents are asked to reach their opposite goals within 60 steps without collision. Agents can choose from moving in four directions and a ’staying’ action. Agents receive 40 marks when both of them reach their goals and -10 marks when they step into the lava. Otherwise, agents receive the marks of their distance to their goals subtracted by 40 after the time step limit. (b): The performance curves of different commonly-used preset TD(λ) values with adaptive λ values. The x-axis is the training time steps (e6) and the y-axis is the final performance. (c) The average adaptive lambda values during the training process.
Figure 5: The winning rate curves evaluated on 27m_vs_30m, 3s_vs_5z, and MMM2 scenarios.
Figure 8: The winning rate curves evaluated on the 6 SMAC tasks with two major difficulties. The baseline algorithms are the QMIX with different commonly-used λ values

Figure 9: The winning rate curves evaluated on the 6 SMAC tasks with two major difficulties. The baseline algorithms are the QMIX with different commonly-used λ values and three methods for adaptive λ calculation methods.
Figure 12: The winning rate curves of our method with/without RNN cells evaluated on 3s5z_vs_3s6z (Super Hard), and 6h_vs_8z (Superhard) scenarios.

Figure 11: (a) The winning rate curves evaluated on MMM2 with different λ cache frequency compared with θ− update. (b) The winning rate curves of our method on single-agent scenario, 1s_vs_1z,. The x-axis is the time steps (1e6) and the y-axis is the average winning rate among 32 different seeds for 10 times experiment.

Figure 7: The difference between predicted Qtot values and the real returns and the two Q values of each agent. The x-axis represents the time steps (1e6) being evaluated and the y-axis is the mean of the winning rate among 10 seeds with 32 evaluation processes.

Figure 2: The utility networks and the mixing network are from the original MARL algorithms, QMIX in this paper. Interactive transitions are stored in two replay buffers. One of them is small (on-policy buffer) and the other one is large (off-policy buffer). Training data are sampled uniformly from these two buffers and used for calculating the likelihood-free density ratios. The density ratios are used as the λ values and importance weights during the training process.
Table 2: Performance in SMAC tasks within 2M time steps

Figure 10: The winning rate curves evaluated on the 6 SMAC tasks with two major difficulties. The x-axis represents the time steps (1e6) being evaluated and the y-axis is the mean of the winning rate among 10 seeds.
